name: Salvadoran Pupusas Revueltas
steps: Mix the masa harina with warm water into a soft, workable dough.
Flatten a ball of dough, fill with the beans, cheese, and pulled pork, then seal and pat back into a disc.
Cook on a hot, dry griddle 3-4 minutes per side until spotted golden brown.
steps_detailed: Mix the masa harina with warm water, a little at a time, kneading until you have a smooth, soft dough that doesn't crack at the edges when pressed - let it rest 5 minutes.
Mash the pinto beans until mostly smooth, and mix the queso fresco and pulled pork together in a separate small bowl.
Divide the dough into balls a bit larger than a golf ball. Flatten each into a disc in your palm, making a slight well in the center.
Spoon a little of the beans and the cheese-and-pork mixture into the well, then fold the edges up and over to seal the filling completely inside.
Gently pat and press the filled ball back out into a flat disc about 1/2 inch thick, patching any tears with a bit of extra dough.
Cook on a lightly oiled hot griddle or skillet over medium heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden-brown spots appear and the pupusa is cooked through.

name: Salvadoran Riguas

ingredients.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps: Blend the corn with the flour and sugar into a thick batter.
Fry spoonfuls in the oil until golden on both sides, about 3-4 minutes per side.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps_detailed: Blend the corn with the flour, sugar, and crumbled queso fresco into a thick, slightly chunky batter.
Fry spoonfuls, flattened slightly, in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until golden and lightly crisp at the edges.
Riguas, sweet fresh corn cakes, are a beloved Salvadoran breakfast, often wrapped in banana leaf while cooking.

name: Salvadoran Fried Plantains and Beans

ingredients.ingredient: Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps: Fry the plantain slices until golden.
Warm the refried beans.
Serve together with a dollop of cream.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps_detailed: Fry the plantain slices in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden.
Warm the refried beans.
Serve everything together with a generous dollop of cream - a classic, comforting Salvadoran breakfast.

name: Salvadoran Semita Bread
steps: Make a simple sweet dough from the flour, butter, and sugar.
Spread with the preserves, top with a second layer of dough, and bake at 350F for 20-25 minutes.
steps_detailed: Make a soft, slightly sweet dough from the flour, butter, sugar, and a splash of water. Divide in half.
Roll out one half, spread with the preserves (traditionally a pineapple filling), and top with the second half, sealing the edges.
Brush with the egg wash and bake at 350F (175C) for 22-28 minutes until golden.
Semita, a layered sweet bread, is a beloved Salvadoran bakery staple.

name: Salvadoran Pupusas de Frijol con Queso

ingredients.ingredient: Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps: Mix the masa harina with warm water into a soft dough.
Flatten a portion, fill with the beans and cheese, and reseal into a ball, then flatten again into a thick disc.
Cook on a hot, lightly oiled griddle, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden with brown spots.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps_detailed: Mix the masa harina with warm water into a soft, pliable dough. Rest 10 minutes.
Flatten a portion of dough into a disc, place a spoonful of beans and cheese in the center, fold the edges up and over to seal, then gently flatten into a thick round again.
Cook on a hot, lightly oiled griddle, 3-5 minutes per side, until golden with characteristic brown spots.
Bean and cheese pupusas are the most classic version of El Salvador's national dish.

name: Salvadoran Sopa de Frijoles
steps: Simmer the beans with the onion and garlic in their liquid plus a splash of water for 15-18 minutes.
Blend partially for a thicker texture.
Top with cilantro and serve with tortillas.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion and garlic until softened, then add the beans with their liquid, cumin, and a splash of water.
Simmer 18-22 minutes, then blend partially with an immersion blender for a thick, rustic texture.
Top with cilantro and crumbled queso fresco, and serve with warm tortillas - a simple, hearty Salvadoran bean soup.

name: Salvadoran Enchiladas Salvadoreñas

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps: Fry the tortilla until crisp.
Brown the beef and pile onto the tortilla with the cabbage and sliced egg.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps_detailed: Fry the tortilla in a little oil until crisp and flat.
Brown the beef, then pile onto the crisp tortilla with the shredded cabbage, sliced egg, and diced tomatoes.
Salvadoran enchiladas, open-faced and topped rather than rolled, are a popular lunch snack distinct from Mexican versions.

name: Salvadoran Panes con Pollo
steps: Season the chicken with the paprika and roast or pan-fry until cooked through.
Shred the chicken and layer into the bread with the cabbage.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken with the paprika and garlic, and roast or pan-fry in the oil until deeply browned and cooked through, 8-10 minutes per side.
Shred the chicken and layer generously into a split roll with the cabbage, tomatoes, and pan juices.
Panes con pollo, a rich shredded chicken sandwich, is a beloved Salvadoran specialty, especially in San Salvador.

name: Salvadoran Tamales Pisques
steps: Mix the masa harina with warm water and the oil into a soft dough.
Spread a thin layer, top with the mashed beans, fold into a packet, and steam 45 minutes.
steps_detailed: Mix the masa harina with warm water, the oil, and cumin into a soft, smooth dough.
Spread a thin layer of dough onto parchment or a soaked corn husk, top with the mashed beans, and fold into a sealed packet.
Steam 45-55 minutes until firm - tamales pisques, filled simply with beans rather than meat, are a beloved everyday Salvadoran staple.

name: Salvadoran Camarones Guisados
steps: Saute the onion and garlic until softened.
Add the tomatoes and simmer 8-10 minutes.
Add the shrimp and simmer 5-6 minutes until just cooked through.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and bell pepper until softened, 5-6 minutes.
Add the tomatoes and simmer 10-12 minutes until thickened.
Add the shrimp and simmer gently 5-7 minutes until just cooked through.
Top with cilantro and serve with rice - a comforting Salvadoran shrimp stew.

name: Salvadoran Curtido
steps: Toss the cabbage and carrot with the vinegar and jalapeno.
Let sit at least 20 minutes to pickle lightly before serving with pupusas.
steps_detailed: Toss the shredded cabbage, carrot, and onion with the vinegar, jalapeno, and cumin.
Season with salt and let sit at least 25-30 minutes so the vegetables lightly pickle.
Curtido is the essential tangy, crunchy topping for pupusas, El Salvador's national dish.

name: Salvadoran Yuca Frita
steps: Boil the cassava until tender, then fry in the oil until golden and crisp.
Serve with the shredded cabbage and chicharron if you have it.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava until tender, 14-18 minutes, then drain and fry in the oil over medium-high heat, 5-7 minutes, until deeply golden and crisp.
Finish with a squeeze of lime and serve with the shredded cabbage - fried yuca is a beloved Salvadoran side and snack.

name: Salvadoran Alboroto

ingredients.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps: Fry the corn kernels in the oil until lightly toasted.
Toss with the honey while still warm.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps_detailed: Fry the corn kernels in the oil over medium heat, stirring frequently, 5-7 minutes until lightly toasted and fragrant.
Toss with the honey and cinnamon while still warm, letting it coat evenly.
Alboroto, sweetened toasted corn, is a traditional Salvadoran treat, especially for All Saints' Day.

name: Salvadoran Empanadas de Platano
steps: Mash the plantain into a smooth dough.
Warm the milk with the sugar into a thick filling.
Wrap the filling in a portion of plantain dough, shape into an oval, and fry until golden.
steps_detailed: Mash the plantain until smooth and dough-like.
Warm the milk with the sugar and cornstarch, stirring, 3-4 minutes until thickened into a custard-like filling. Let cool slightly.
Take a portion of the plantain dough, flatten, spoon the filling into the center, and fold closed into a small oval.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, turning, 4-6 minutes until deeply golden - empanadas de platano, filled with sweet milk custard, are a beloved Salvadoran dessert.

name: Salvadoran Chilate

ingredients.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps: Blend the corn with the milk until smooth.
Simmer with the sugar and cinnamon for 8-10 minutes until slightly thickened.
Serve warm.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps_detailed: Blend the corn with the milk until very smooth.
Simmer with the sugar and cinnamon over medium-low heat, stirring constantly, 10-12 minutes until slightly thickened.
Serve warm - chilate, a spiced corn drink, is a traditional Salvadoran beverage often paired with sweet bread.

name: Salvadoran Casamiento
steps: Saute the onion in the oil until softened.
Add the rice and beans, and stir-fry 4-5 minutes until well combined.
Serve as a side.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion and garlic in the oil over medium heat, 4-5 minutes, until softened.
Add the rice and beans, and stir-fry 5-6 minutes until well combined and lightly toasted.
Casamiento, rice and beans cooked together, is a beloved Salvadoran side, often served alongside eggs or fried plantain.

name: Salvadoran Quesadilla Salvadoreña
steps: Mix the flour with the crumbled queso fresco, sugar, and egg into a smooth batter.
Pour into a small buttered dish and bake at 350F for 25-30 minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, crumbled queso fresco, sugar, and egg into a smooth, pourable batter.
Pour into a small buttered baking dish and scatter with sesame seeds.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 26-32 minutes until golden and set.
Despite the name, Salvadoran quesadilla is a sweet, dense cheese cake, entirely different from the Mexican savory dish - a beloved bakery treat.

name: Salvadoran Tamales de Elote

ingredients.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps: Blend the corn with the masa harina, sugar, and coconut milk into a thick batter.
Wrap portions in foil and steam 35-40 minutes until firm.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps_detailed: Blend the corn with the masa harina, sugar, coconut milk, and cinnamon into a thick, cohesive batter.
Wrap portions tightly in foil (traditionally corn husk) and steam 38-45 minutes until firm and set.
Sweet corn tamales are a treasured Salvadoran treat, especially popular around the holidays.

name: Salvadoran Salsa Roja
steps: Simmer the tomatoes with the garlic and jalapeno for 10-12 minutes until thickened.
Serve alongside pupusas or grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and jalapeno in the oil briefly, then add the tomatoes.
Simmer 12-15 minutes until thickened into a smooth, savory sauce - a common accompaniment to pupusas alongside curtido.

name: Salvadoran Crema Salvadoreña
steps: Whisk the cream cheese with the milk and lime juice until smooth and pourable.
Serve drizzled over pupusas, beans, or fried plantains.
steps_detailed: Whisk the cream cheese with the milk and lime juice until smooth and pourable, thinning with a touch more milk if needed.
Crema, tangy and rich, tops nearly everything in Salvadoran cooking.
